title: Born in the Heart of the Delta
summary: Interviewed by Emilye Crosby at the Library of Congress in December 2015, Freddie Greene Biddle opens with her birth in Greenwood, Mississippi, on February 15, 1945, a large Black family, a father who ran a house-painting business and had been involved with the NAACP, and a mother who taught school until marrying at twenty-nine and found her father's farm too quiet and dark after a Greenwood girlhood.

title: A Horse, a Wagon, and a Lost Brother
summary: She recounts her mother's only sibling killed at five when a tethered horse kicked him in the head in front of the house, leaving her mother an over-protected only child who went to Wesley Methodist Church and to Tougaloo for high school and college, and the rare standing of a Black family in Leflore County that owned its inherited home.

title: He Did Not Want to Farm
summary: Biddle describes her father, born in 1900, going to Alcorn and then a year at Tuskegee Institute to study shoemaking because he wanted to work for himself rather than farm, and the small pockets of land-owning Black families around his rural home where people lived off the land and helped one another.

title: An Act of Faith in Social Security
summary: She tells of grandparents dying around 1951 and 1952, an uncle who was one of two Black postmen in Greenwood, and her father's conviction, shared with friend Mr. Hilton, that paying into Social Security when no one around him had ever experienced it was an act of faith that would support him in old age.

title: Retired by the Movement
summary: Biddle explains that the Social Security her father trusted since the 1930s proved its worth when the family's movement involvement dried up his painting work, forcing him to retire on it in 1964 at the very age he became eligible, an advantage she credits to having older parents who could withstand the economic pressure.

title: On the Edge of the Black Community
summary: She maps the 600 block of Howard Street where she grew up, separated from downtown Greenwood by a railroad track and bordering the white neighborhood close enough to hear white children at the park, and her father's grievance that his tax dollars bused county white children to better schools.

title: Hand-Me-Down Textbooks
summary: Biddle recalls schools wholly segregated from 1951 through her 1963 graduation, textbooks shipped over from the white school once white children got new ones, and how after Brown Mississippi built high schools in small communities like Greenwood and later a county school, Amanda Elzy, for rural children.

title: I Could Not Name a White Person
summary: She describes a childhood of window shopping downtown only with parents, a corner store her father forbade, white people across the street picking up domestic workers and laundry, the white superintendent opening commencement, and the white football team brought to special bleachers, with so little contact that she could not name a single white person she grew up alongside.

title: A Body Brought to Greenwood
summary: She recalls a body brought to a Greenwood funeral home that none wanted to handle, her mother's stories of a person burned alive, and her father's warnings about a dry state's bootleggers who, to stay in business, fed information to the white downtown and would make up stories on people.

title: North Greenwood and Shotgun Houses
summary: Biddle contrasts wealthy old white families in North Greenwood tracing roots to the plantations, a sizable Jewish community, and Chinese families who ran small grocery stores in the Black community while their children attended the white school, against the shotgun houses on brick stacks with planks laid over open sewers and her own older frame home on Howard Street.

title: Antebellum Homes and GI Houses
summary: Biddle contrasts the antebellum houses of North Greenwood with the ordinary white community's frame homes, and a small early-1960s housing development across from the high school where returning veterans could finally finance a four-room house, an improvement over crowded shotgun rentals whose wood deteriorated unmaintained.

title: Take Back the Application
summary: She tells how her father had long worked with the NAACP and her parents registered to vote in the late 1950s without voting, and how in 1962 her brother Dwight, back from the Navy and wanting more than the education major Mississippi allowed Black students, applied to be the second Black student at Ole Miss, drawing the mayor's demand that he withdraw it.

title: The Mississippi Free Press
summary: Biddle recounts Dwight going to work for the Mississippi Free Press in Jackson and through it meeting Robert Parris Moses, Sam Block, Willie Peacock, Reggie Robinson, Medgar Evers, and Amzie Moore of Cleveland, and how Willie Peacock and Sam Block came into Greenwood in fall 1962 to hold mass meetings that opened with Hollis Watkins singing to move people toward registering to vote.

title: Dick Gregory Sent a Plane
summary: Biddle explains that as cotton-picking machines arrived in 1962 and Leflore County cut off the surplus food, powdered milk and peanut butter that struggling people needed, her father saw it as a deliberate effort to starve people for registering to vote, and recalls Dick Gregory renting a plane and Ivanhoe Donaldson driving a truck to bring food, distributed from Wesley Methodist Church where she helped bag and box it.

title: An Outside World
summary: She describes the SNCC workers as an exposure to a world beyond Mississippi, Stokely Carmichael telling her of New York and Trinidad, in a town where the public library was closed to Black residents, and meeting Eleanor Holmes and Tim Jenkins as a high school senior, while naming local supporters like June Johnson's mother, the McGee family, and Aaron Johnson, whose First Christian Church the white community forced him to rename.

title: Shotgun Blast Through the Front Door
summary: Biddle describes teachers too terrified of losing their jobs to take part, a March 1963 shotgun blast through her family's front door after George attended a SNCC meeting, schoolmates' parents who kept their children away for fear, and her own departure that September for Dillard University in New Orleans to study nursing, alongside cultural events like Pete Seeger and the Freedom Singers performing in Mississippi.

part: Going Away While the Movement Burned
title: My Brother in Parchman Farm
summary: Biddle describes the Free Southern Theater touring Mississippi, and how hard it was to leave for college just after big Greenwood demonstrations where police dogs she could hear from her house arrested George and others on trespassing charges and sent them to Parchman Farm, and James Forman, a mentor she loved, urging her to go because school would let her do more when she returned.

title: Nobody at Dillard Cared
summary: She recalls being at Dillard when the Birmingham church bombing killed the four girls, and after Medgar Evers and the shots into her own house it seemed nothing would stop, hard to study amid a campus with no student movement, until she connected with Casey Hayden and a Tulane group through the Newman Center under New Orleans's strict girls' rules.

title: George Talks Dad Into McComb
summary: Biddle tells how, hearing of the 1964 summer project from George and wanting to go somewhere other than Greenwood, she got her parents to agree she could work in McComb where George had a project, went to the Ohio orientation and on to Washington with the Southwest Mississippi group to ask congressional representatives for protection, and was there when word came that Schwerner, Chaney, and Goodman had been kidnapped.

title: Half the House Was Taken Away
summary: Biddle describes eleven people sharing a McComb freedom house split between freedom schools and canvassing, a staff meeting the night before in which everyone but George voted to bring down more volunteers, and the dynamite set at the garage door that shattered windows two blocks away and embedded glass in Curtis, sleeping nearby, as they woke to shouts of get down and crawled toward the half-gone house.

title: A Minister Visits the Heffners
summary: Biddle tells how George drove Curtis to Jackson to see a doctor and how walking the McComb streets to keep the community calm they saw people closing their doors in fear, and recounts a minister volunteer arranging a visit to the Heffner family, sending in the blonde Stanford student Dennis Sweeney to pass as kin, after which the Heffners were driven from town, while no white person spoke out though nine Black churches were burned.

part: Atlantic City and the Fairground Jail
title: We Showed It Was Possible
summary: She describes canvassing and holding meetings to select Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party delegates that short summer, going with them to the Atlantic City convention, her father's pride that they showed the country it was possible whether or not they were seated, Fannie Lou Hamer's speech reaching the whole world, and her own realization when the seats were refused that Mississippi acted as it did because the rest of the country did not care.

title: Education Was Not Enough
summary: Biddle recounts her hardest year back at Dillard as classmates urged her to finish school and get a good job, her argument that people lacked education because the system was structured to deny it, and her decision at the end of her second year that she could not return to Dillard or to nursing because real change demanded decent housing, healthcare, and opportunity, not education alone.

title: The Fairground Penitentiary
summary: Biddle describes leaving Dillard to work on voter registration in Jackson, where 1965 demonstrations led authorities to turn the fairground into a penitentiary, jailing Black demonstrators on a concrete floor under a tent while white women went to the actual jail, and her role as a later arrival, going in with Janet Moses, to lift the morale of people held for weeks not knowing when they would be released.

title: Ruby Doris Said Learn Everything
summary: Biddle tells of moving to the Atlanta SNCC office after jail, drawn partly by James Forman who mixed field and office staff and put her on the switchboard, and of working closely with Ruby Doris Smith Robinson, a Spelman graduate, Freedom Rider, and one of her heroes who could outwork anyone and urged her to learn as many things as she could, drawing her into finance though a Mississippi girl had never imagined a business career.

title: When Black Power Came Out
topic: Finance and the Money Drying Up
summary: Biddle describes helping SNCC bookkeeper Chessie Johnson with finances and donations while George stayed in the field, canvassing in Lowndes County, Alabama, and recounts being in the Atlanta office as Stokely Carmichael raised the cry of Black Power on the Meredith March Against Fear in 1966, after which donations sharply fell and the media pressed SNCC about whether it meant a separate nation.

title: Problems Better Solved by Black Staff
summary: Biddle gives the staff's reasoning that problems in the Black community would be better solved by Black staff while white staff could do more in the white community, the press inflating it, and notes she stayed on staff until June 1968 after King was killed in April, by which time the money was gone.
related_events: Assassination of Martin Luther King Jr.

title: You're Going to Be Sorry
summary: She describes Stokely Carmichael, tall, dark, and personable, brought home around 1962 and a regular guest at her family's house whenever he was in Greenwood, telling her of his Howard students in NAG and teasing that she was making a mistake going to McComb because he would flood the Fourth Congressional District with his people, and how none of the college students could persuade her to leave the state.

title: They Said They Were My Sisters
summary: Biddle recalls meeting Joyce and Dorie Ann Ladner, also Mississippians, and a night when Stokely Carmichael and Dorie Ann Ladner, down for Mardi Gras, talked the Dillard housemother into letting her come down by claiming to be her sisters and brothers, leaving her overjoyed at the way movement people made connections.

title: Not for Personal Advancement
summary: She closes on the movement's power to draw people from all walks of life, recalling that Robert Parris Moses, James Forman, and everyone she encountered tried to help others and to expose people to making a difference, none of them seeking personal advancement but setting aside their own possibilities to help somebody else.
